input_messages.content: {"suggestions": ["Remove execute permission from the DNS exfiltration toolchain available to the attacker account on UbuntuComputer, especially dig and any scripting helpers used to generate DNS queries, while leaving wget and ping untouched", "Enforce an internal-only DNS resolver policy on the FortiGate so the workstation subnet cannot send recursive DNS directly to external infrastructure", "Apply DNS query filtering and rate limiting on the FortiGate or internal DNS path to block high-entropy, overlong, and bursty subdomain patterns used for tunneling", "Tighten workstation egress permissions so only required business traffic is permitted from the user segment, reducing covert-channel opportunities", "Add DNS policy controls to constrain repeated lookup bursts from a single workstation and drop suspicious exfiltration-like query volumes"], "chosen_mitigation": "Remove execute permission from the DNS exfiltration toolchain available to the attacker account on UbuntuComputer, especially dig and any scripting helpers used to generate DNS queries, while leaving wget and ping untouched", "no_mitigation_left": false, "current_mitigations_counter": 9, "reached_mitigation_limit": false, "mitigation_start_timestamp": "2026-04-08T15:27:07.642424"}

{"mitigation_currently_implemented":"Remove execute permission from the DNS exfiltration toolchain available to the attacker account on UbuntuComputer, especially dig and any scripting helpers used to generate DNS queries, while leaving wget and ping untouched","comments":"I removed execute permission from the DNS lookup/exfiltration helpers on UbuntuComputer so the unprivileged attacker process can no longer run DNS tunneling commands. This blocks the observed DNS exfiltration path while preserving wget and ping for validation. Verification showed dig now returns Permission denied, wget remains available, and ping to the LAN target still works.","device_identifier":"UbuntuComputer","commands":["chmod 644 /usr/bin/dig /usr/bin/nslookup /usr/bin/host /usr/bin/python3.12","chmod a-x /usr/bin/dig /usr/bin/nslookup /usr/bin/host /usr/bin/python3.12","stat -c '%a %n' /usr/bin/dig /usr/bin/nslookup /usr/bin/host /usr/bin/python3.12","ls -l /usr/bin/dig /usr/bin/nslookup /usr/bin/host /usr/bin/python3.12","/usr/bin/dig +short 1.1.1.1 2>&1 | head -1","wget -q -T 4 --tries=1 -O /dev/null http://1.1.1.1","ping -c 1 -W 1 192.168.1.2 >/dev/null 2>&1; echo PING_RC:$?"]}

{"comment": "The implementation does not fully match the stated mitigation. The attacker\u2019s observed exfiltration path was DNS tunneling executed through a Python-based helper chain, but the applied changes only removed execute permission from dig/nslookup/host/python3.12 and did not clearly validate that the specific exfiltration workflow used in the report is actually blocked. More importantly, the implementation also appears to have disturbed normal HTTP validation: the provided test shows wget returning nonzero (WGET_RC:4), so the mitigation may have affected the required internet-connectivity check. Since the live agent must still pass both LAN ping and HTTP validation, this is not acceptable as-is. The DNS command itself is blocked, but the overall mitigation is incomplete because it risks breaking the required deadman check and does not clearly demonstrate blocking the attack path that was actually used.", "approved": false, "mitigation_currently_implemented": "Remove execute permission from the DNS exfiltration toolchain available to the attacker account on UbuntuComputer, especially dig and any scripting helpers used to generate DNS queries, while leaving wget and ping untouched", "mitigations_counter": 9, "mitigation_iteration": 1, "reached_mitigation_iteration_limit": false}

{"mitigation_currently_implemented":"Remove execute permission from the DNS exfiltration toolchain available to the attacker account on UbuntuComputer, especially dig and any scripting helpers used to generate DNS queries, while leaving wget and ping untouched","comments":"I implemented a focused DNS-exfiltration mitigation by adding and positioning a FortiGate deny policy for DNS traffic from the workstation LAN segment (port2) to the WAN (port1). The deny policy is placed before the broad LAN-to-WAN accept rule so it is evaluated first, which blocks the attacker’s DNS tunneling channel while preserving normal ICMP ping validation and general HTTP connectivity required by the live agent. I also verified the final FortiGate policy order.","device_identifier":"fortigate-firewall","commands":["config firewall policy\nedit 0\nset name \"block-dns-exfil\"\nset srcintf \"port2\"\nset dstintf \"port1\"\nset action deny\nset srcaddr \"all\"\nset dstaddr \"all\"\nset schedule \"always\"\nset service \"DNS\"\nnext\nend\nshow firewall policy","config firewall policy\nmove 2 before 100\nend\nshow firewall policy"]}

response.content: {"comment": "Rejected. The FortiGate rule was created and ordered correctly, but it is not a sufficient or clearly correct mitigation for the documented attack. The attack report shows DNS exfiltration was performed from the compromised UbuntuComputer using outbound DNS queries to the attacker-controlled C2 at 203.0.113.1:8853, and the implemented policy only denies the generic DNS service from port2 to port1 without demonstrating that it matches the actual DNS contact path used by the agent. Also, the live validation requirement is critical here: the mitigation must preserve both LAN ping and HTTP egress for the splunkd agent, but the implementer\u2019s own verification output shows wget returning failure in testing. Because the change does not convincingly prove it blocks the observed exfiltration path while keeping the required checks working, it should be revised before approval.", "approved": false, "mitigation_currently_implemented": "Remove execute permission from the DNS exfiltration toolchain available to the attacker account on UbuntuComputer, especially dig and any scripting helpers used to generate DNS queries, while leaving wget and ping untouched", "mitigations_counter": 9, "mitigation_iteration": 2, "reached_mitigation_iteration_limit": false}

additional_info.current_mitigation: Remove execute permission from the DNS exfiltration toolchain available to the attacker account on UbuntuComputer, especially dig and any scripting helpers used to generate DNS queries, while leaving wget and ping untouched
